person.userCertifiedLifeSummary:
According to the book, "The history of Peter Parker and Sarah Ruggles of Roxbury..." (see Sources), Benjamin's name first appears in Massachusetts records in 1651 when he witnessed a deed between two gentlemen in Roxbury. I have seen on the internet where some people say this means he was 21 years old in 1651, but that is not true.  You only had to be 14 to witness a deed, and sometimes children were used as witnesses because it was hoped they could outlive the parties to the transaction.  

Roxbury is a dissolved municipality and is no longer a town, but just a neighborhood in Boston.  It was originally part of Suffolk County, and then became part of Norfolk County when Norfolk County was created, and then became part of Suffolk County again when it became part of Boston.  The settlers of Roxbury originally comprised the congregation of the First Church in Roxbury, established in 1632.  Benjamin was not among these first inhabitants, but apparently came later before 1651. 

Benjamin died intestate, but there are probate records related to his estate, which mention his death date.  In 1690, three men were appointed to inspect all "accounts of management" in order to prevent differences.  The appointment was signed by Mary Child [the widow], Benjamin Child, Mary's husband Jacob Chamberlain, John Child, Elizabeth Child, and Mehittabell Child.  According to this same source, a deed dated 1699 regarding the settlement of Benjamin's estate was signed by Jacob Chamberlain and Mary his wife, Elizabeth Child Spinster, Samuel Perrin and Mehetiable his wife, and John Child on the one part, and by Benjamin Child on the other part.    Apparently another similar quit claim was also signed by Joshua Child. 

Although most of the children remained in Roxbury, both John and Mehitable were early settlers of Woodstock, Connecticut, and some of his grandchildren moved there as well.  

King Philip's War was an armed conflict between American Indian inhabitants of present-day New England and English colonists and their Indian allies in 1675–78. It appears that Benjamin's oldest son was killed in this war.  Early in King Philip's War Captain Richard Beers and 36 mounted soldiers were ambushed by 150 Indians near Northfield, MA. Capt Beers and nineteen of his men were killed, and sixteen of the soldiers managed to escape. See Ephraim's file for more sources.  

Benjamin and his son Ephraim are mentioned in the will of Deacon Ephraim Child of Watertown, dated 1662.  Benjamin and Deacon Ephraim's exact relationship, it appears, has been the subject of some controversy.  Some say brother, and others say nephew/uncle.  I am not sure this has been satisfactorily resolved.  This same source gives this Benjamin's father as Benjamin, but provides no proof whatsoever.  

According to The Great Migration series by noted author and historian Robert Charles Anderson, Ephraim was in Watertown in 1630, went back to England for business in 1647 and returned in 1648.  He had no known children and left his estate to a variety of others, including this Benjamin's son also named Ephraim.  Deacon Ephraim's wife Elizabeth mentioned this Benjamin's wife in his will, leaving her a silk handkerchief and hood.  Citing TAG 63: 17-28, Anderson in this source gives Deacon Ephraim's parents as Wolstone and Ellen Child and wife as Elizabeth Palmer, widow, formerly Elizabeth Bond.  They married 8 February 1624/5 in Nayland, Suffolk.  According to Anderson, Deacon Ephraim moved to Nayland as an adult, but no indication is given as to where he moved from.   

Elias Child, author of the book "Genealogy of the Child, Childs, and Childe families : of the past and present in the United States and the Canadas, from 1630..." published in 1881 had a chapter dedicated to Deacon Ephraim Child and this Benjamin Child.  He was unable, in this book, to shed any light on where Ephraim and Benjamin may have hailed from in England.  Elias mentions in his book that Benjamin had 12 children; I have only found evidence for 11.  He also mentioned that Benjamin Junior was this Benjamin's second child; I think he was probably his third child.  His headstone indicated that Benjamin Junior was in his 66th year when he died in 1724, giving him an estimated birth year of 1659.  Joshua's indicated that he died in his 73rd year in 1730, giving him an estimated birth year of 1658.  

1. Ephraim - about 1654
*2. Benjamin - about 1659
3. Joshua - about 1658
4. Mary - b. 8 August 1660
5. Infant - b. 1662
6. Elizabeth - b. 2 December 1663
7. Margaret -b. 21 December 1665
8. John - b. 8 January 1667
9. Mehitable - b. 29 June 1669
10. John -  b. 1 August 1671
11. Joseph - b. 1 June 1673
12. Joseph - b. 10 December 1674

Elias gives no maiden name for this Benjamin's wife Mary.  See her file for evidence that her maiden name was "Bowen."

There is a christening record attached to Benjamin's file from Cotesbach, Leicestershire.  Although I haven't seen this christening date in any published sources, I think it has real potential, as John Dillingham and Edward Dillingham, early settlers of Boston, are known as also being from Cotesbach.  See The Great Migration series.

zh.lifeSummary: 當Benjamin Child於1631年1月15日在英國萊希斯特斯爾可斯巴賀聖 馬也斯 邱奇受洗時，他的父親Benjamin Childe38歲，母親Sarah Shenton21歲。他於大約1653年與Mary Bowen在布利提申 可隆尼亞爾 亞美利加馬薩瑟次 貝 可隆尼素福爾可波斯頓結婚。他們至少是7個兒子和4個女兒的父母。他去世於1678年10月14日在布利提申 可隆尼亞爾 亞美利加馬薩瑟次 貝 可隆尼素福爾可洛波利享年47歲，埋葬在布利提申 可隆尼亞爾 亞美利加馬薩瑟次 貝 可隆尼素福爾可波斯頓洛波利艾略特 波英 格洛德。

nameMeanings.name: Child
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: nickname from Middle English child ‘child, infant’ (Old English cild), in various possible applications. The word is found in Old English as a byname, and in Middle English as a widely used affectionate term of address. It was also used as a term of status for a young man of noble birth, although the exact meaning is not clear; in the 13th and 14th centuries it was a technical term used of a young noble awaiting elevation to the knighthood. In other cases it may have been applied as a byname to a youth considerably younger than his brothers or to one who was a minor on the death of his father.
in Kent, possibly a topographic name from Old English cielde ‘spring (water)’, a rare word derived from c(e)ald ‘cold’.

variants.name: Barnes
meanings: English: habitational name from Barnes (on the Surrey bank of the Thames in London), named with Old English bere-ærn ‘barn, a storehouse for barley and other grain’, or a topographic name or metonymic occupational name for someone who lived by or worked at a barn or barns, from Middle English barn ‘barn, granary’.

English: variant of Barne, with excrescent -s, derived from either the Middle English personal name Bern, Barn (based on the Scandinavian personal name Biǫrn or Old English Beorn, both from a word meaning ‘warrior’), or from Middle English barn (Old Norse barn) ‘child’. The latter term is found as a byname for men of the upper classes; it might also have had the meaning ‘young man of a prominent family’, like Middle English child (see Child ).
Irish: in Ireland in many cases this is no doubt the English name, but in others it is possibly an Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Bearáin ‘descendant of Bearán’, a byname meaning ‘spear’.

variants.name: Chill
meanings: English (Staffordshire and Derbyshire): variant of   Child   or   Chell  .

variants.name: Childs
meanings: English: variant of Child , with post-medieval excrescent -s.

variants.name: Chilton
meanings: English: habitational name from any of various places called Chilton, for example in Berkshire, Buckinghamshire, County Durham, Hampshire, Kent, Shropshire, Somerset, Suffolk, and Wiltshire. The majority are shown by early forms to derive from Old English cild ‘child’ (see Child ) + tūn ‘enclosure, settlement’. One place of this name in Somerset possibly gets its first element from Old English cealc ‘chalk, limestone’, and one on the Isle of Wight from the personal name Cēola (compare Chilcott ), or from Old English ceole ‘deep valley’.

variants.name: Bambino
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Carmine, Angelo.

Italian: from Bambino, a byname meaning ‘child’ (see Child ), or a personal name given with reference to Gesù Bambino ‘the Christ child’, the use of Gesù as a personal name being considered taboo in Italy.

variants.name: Chilson
meanings: English: habitational name from Chilson in Oxfordshire, named with Old English cild ‘young man’ (see Child ) + tūn ‘farmstead, settlement’.

History: It is not known when this surname was first brought to America, but it was already established in CT in the early 18th century. Daniel Chilson of Weathersfield, CT, was born c. 1720 and on 4 October 1745 married Sybil Stanclift in Middlesex County, CT.

variants.name: Fairchild
meanings: English (southern): nickname from Middle English fair child ‘handsome child’ or ‘handsome youth of noble birth’ (see Child ).

variants.name: Childers
meanings: English: probably a habitational name from some lost place called Childerhouse, from Old English cildra, genitive plural of cild ‘child’ + hūs ‘house’. This may have referred to some form of orphanage perhaps run by a religious order, or perhaps the first element is to be understood in its later sense as a term of status (see Child ).

nameMeanings.name: Benjamin
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Biblical name, borne by the youngest of the twelve sons of Jacob. His mother Rachel died in giving birth to him, and in her last moments she named him Benoni, meaning ‘son of my sorrow’. His father, however, did not wish him to bear such an ill-omened name, and renamed him Benyamin (Genesis 35:16–18; 42:4). This means either ‘son of the right hand’ or more likely ‘son of the south’ (Hebrew yamin can also mean ‘south’), since Benjamin was the only child of Jacob born in Canaan and not in Mesopotamia to the north. Another tradition is that the second element of the name is a variant of the Hebrew plural noun yamim, which means ‘days’ but is used idiomatically to mean ‘year’ or ‘years’. The name would then mean ‘son of (my) old age’ and refer to the fact that Benjamin was Jacob's youngest child. In the Middle Ages the name was often given to sons whose mothers had died in childbirth. Today it has no such unfortunate associations and it grew enormously in popularity following the release of the film The Graduate ( 1967 ), in which Dustin Hoffman played the role of Benjamin Braddock . It is used in Scotland as an Anglicized form of Gaelic Beathan .

historicEvents.title: The English Civil War
historicEvents.description: A series of conflicts regarding England's governance during the years 1642 to 1651 is now known as The English Civil War. Charles I summoned supporters to join him against his enemies in Parliament. In October 1642, nearly 10,000 men fought for Charles I and chased Parliament across the River Tamar. Fighting continued for years and was finally ended at the Battle of Worcester on September 3, 1651, with a Parliamentarian victory.

en.lifeSummary: When Benjamin Child was christened on 15 January 1631, in St Mary's Church, Cotesbach, Leicestershire, England, his father, Benjamin Childe, was 38 and his mother, Sarah Shenton, was 21. He married Mary Bowen about 1653, in Boston, Suffolk, Massachusetts Bay Colony, British Colonial America. They were the parents of at least 7 sons and 4 daughters. He died on 14 October 1678, in Roxbury, Suffolk, Massachusetts Bay Colony, British Colonial America, at the age of 47, and was buried in Eliot Burying Ground, Roxbury, Boston, Suffolk, Massachusetts Bay Colony, British Colonial America.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Benjamin Child foi batizado em 15 de janeiro de 1631, em St Mary's Church, Cotesbach, Leicestershire, Inglaterra, seu pai, Benjamin Childe, tinha 38 anos e sua mãe, Sarah Shenton, tinha 21 anos. Ele casou-se com Mary Bowen aproximadamente 1653, em Boston, Suffolk, Massachusetts Bay Colony, British Colonial America. Eles tiveram pelo menos 7 filhos e 4 filhas. Ele faleceu em 14 de outubro de 1678, em Roxbury, Suffolk, Massachusetts Bay Colony, British Colonial America, com 47 anos, e foi sepultado em Eliot Burying Ground, Roxbury, Boston, Suffolk, Massachusetts Bay Colony, British Colonial America.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Benjamin Child был крещен 15 января 1631 г. в местности ст мэри'с чурч, котесбач, лисестиршир, Англия, его отцу по имени Benjamin Childe было 38 лет, а матери, которую звали Sarah Shenton, 21. Он женился на женщине по имени Mary Bowen около 1653 г. в местности бостон, суффолк, массачусетц беи колони, бритиш колоняль амирика. У них было по крайней мере 7 сыновей и 4 дочери. Он умер 14 октября 1678 г. в местности роксбури, суффолк, массачусетц беи колони, бритиш колоняль амирика в возрасте 47 лет и был похоронен в местности элиот буринг гроунд, роксбури, бостон, суффолк, массачусетц беи колони, бритиш колоняль амирика.
